actions.thought: 9s 4h is a very weak starting hand with no potential for high-value combinations or effective bluffing equity. As the first player to act, there is no strategic reason to invest chips into the pot with this holding. Folding is the optimal choice to preserve my stack and wait for a more playable hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Hearts and Five of Clubs. The Button has folded. While it's only a 50-chip call to see a flop, Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Against a competent opponent in the Big Blind, completing or raising with this hand is generally unprofitable in the long run. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to engage.

I am on the button with 96s, which is a solid speculative hand in a 3-handed game. Opening for a raise allows me to take control of the hand, put pressure on the blinds, and utilize my positional advantage post-flop. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.
Facing a 3x raise from the button, T8o is much too weak to play from the Small Blind out of position. Folding is the only viable option.

Holding 7h 9h in the big blind against a button open is a standard call. The pot odds are favorable (200 to call into a 450 pot), and suited connectors offer good post-flop equity. Folding would be too tight, and raising is not optimal given the stack depth and the relative strength of my hand out of position.

I hold a flush draw on a dry board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play to control the pot size and realize my equity. I will assess the opponent's reaction to my check and proceed accordingly.

I have middle pair (sixes) on a Qh-3s-6h board after being the pre-flop aggressor. While middle pair has showdown value and can often be checked back for pot control, betting here allows me to protect against overcards and heart draws while extracting value from worse hands like a 3 or a lower pocket pair. A bet of roughly half the pot is a standard continuation bet that maintains pressure and defines my opponent's range.

I hold a flush draw on a Queen-high board. While I am out of position, the pot odds provided by the opponent's bet make a call mathematically justifiable. Additionally, the potential for implied odds should I hit my flush on the turn makes continuing the hand the best strategic path. Folding is too tight, and raising is a riskier play given the opponent's demonstrated strength on this board.

I am out of position with a flush draw on a Queen-high board. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size and realize my equity, as leading into the pre-flop aggressor could lead to being blown off my equity. I will check to see the opponent's action and evaluate my pot odds for a potential call.

I have middle pair (sixes) on a board with an overcard (Queen). After my continuation bet was called on the flop, the turn 2s is relatively safe but doesn't improve my hand. Checking back here is a standard play for pot control and allows me to reach a showdown more cheaply with a hand that has some value but is vulnerable to a check-raise. It also protects my range and could induce a bluff on the river from missed heart or spade draws.

I missed my flush draw and ended up with nine-high on a board paired with queens. My hand has no showdown value, and my opponent has shown strength by betting the flop, even if they checked the turn. A bluff with nine-high is unlikely to succeed against a calling range that likely contains a queen or even a pocket pair, and checking is the most prudent way to minimize losses.

On the river, the second Queen appears, giving me two pair (Queens and Sixes). Flashlight has checked for the third consecutive street after calling my flop continuation bet and seeing a check on the turn. My pair of sixes has significant showdown value in a three-handed game, especially against missed draws (hearts and spades) or small pairs (like a 3 or small pocket pairs). While I could consider a small value bet, it is unlikely a worse hand calls, and checking back ensures I reach showdown with a winning hand most of the time without risking a check-raise.
43 offsuit is a very weak hand with extremely low equity and playability. As the first to act pre-flop, folding is the standard and optimal play.

With Jack-Queen offsuit in the small blind, I am heads-up against the big blind after the button folded. This is a strong hand in this situation, and raising to 300 allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or exert pressure going into the flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9c 5c. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more to see the flop. In a Blind vs Blind situation, 95s is a strong enough hand to defend, especially since I will have position on Flashlight for the remainder of the hand. The pot odds are 2:1, which makes this a standard call to realize equity and play post-flop.

I have top pair with a good kicker on a relatively safe board. Betting 300, which is half the pot, allows me to extract value from weaker holdings and maintain control of the hand while charging any speculative hands. Checking might be too passive given I raised pre-flop and have the initiative.

Flashlight made a standard half-pot continuation bet on a relatively dry board of Qc 8h 4s. I have 9c 5c, which missed this flop entirely. While I have a backdoor flush draw, I lack a pair or any direct straight draw. Calling 300 into a 900 pot (offering 3:1 odds) requires significantly more equity or a clearer path to winning the hand than 9-high currently provides. Against a pre-flop raiser's range, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Opening 7c8s from the button is a profitable play in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at 97 big blinds, and this hand has decent connectivity and potential to take down the blinds or play in position. I will use a standard open raise size of 300 to maintain consistency with my previous opening range.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for 300. T7o is a weak hand to defend out of position, especially with the Big Blind still to act. Calling often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I am out of position with a marginal hand. Given the stack depths and the early stage of the tournament, folding is the most prudent play to preserve chips for better opportunities.
Facing a 3bb open from the Button, we hold K2o in the Big Blind. This hand is completely dominated, unsuited, and generally plays terribly post-flop out of position. It's a clear fold.

I am on the button with Queen-Four offsuit. While being on the button provides a positional advantage, Q4o is a very weak hand and does not meet the standard requirements for an opening raise in a three-handed game. Given the early stage of the tournament (Hand 5 of 150) and the lack of specific reads on the blinds' folding frequencies, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

With Q5s in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a playable suited hand. Completing the bet (limping) is a standard play here to see a flop cheaply against the Big Blind without overcommitting with a marginal holding.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d4d. The Small Blind has completed the bet to match my 100. I have the option to check and see a free flop, which is the standard play with a speculative suited connector like 34s. Raising would be unnecessary aggression in this spot, so I will check to see the flop.

I have Queen high with a backdoor flush draw on a paired Ace board. Being out of position against the Big Blind, the best move is to check. There is no reason to lead out as an Ace is well within the BB's checking range pre-flop, and I can easily fold to a bet.

I have flopped trips with 3d4d on a board of 3h-Ac-3s. This is a very dry board, and the opponent has checked. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands and might fold out bluffs. Checking allows me to control the pot size, induce potential bluffs on the turn, and keep the opponent's range wide.

I turned a pair of 5s, giving me two pair (5s and 3s) with an Ace kicker. This hand has marginal showdown value. Betting here would likely only get called by better hands like an unexpectedly slow-played Ace, a 3, or a strong draw, while folding out weaker hands. Checking allows me to control the pot size out of position and potentially catch a bluff from the Big Blind.

I have flopped trips with 3d4d on a board of 3h-Ac-3s-5h. The opponent has checked the flop and now checked the turn. With a strong hand like trip threes, I need to extract value from any Ace or weaker pair the opponent may be holding. Checking back would lose potential value, so I will bet half the pot to build the pot and get paid.

I turned a pair of 5s on an A-3-3-5 board, giving me two pair (5s and 3s) with an Ace kicker. My opponent checked back the flop, making it somewhat unlikely they have an Ace or a 3. Their half-pot turn bet of 100 into a 200 pot could easily be a flush draw, a straight draw, a weaker 5, or simply a delayed bluff. Given the 3:1 pot odds and my decent showdown value, calling is the most standard and profitable play. Raising would overplay my hand and isolate myself against stronger hands.

On the river, the board completes to A-3-3-5-6 with no flush possible. I have a pair of 5s. Acting first, leading out here would turn my marginal showdown value hand into a bluff or a very thin value bet, which will mostly be called by better hands like an Ace, a 6, or a higher pocket pair. By checking, I control the pot size and allow my opponent to either check back with weaker holdings or attempt a bluff, which I can then evaluate.

I have flopped trip threes with 3d-4d on a board of 3h-Ac-3s-5h-6d. The opponent called my turn bet, suggesting they have an Ace or a weaker pair. With the board having paired threes and a relatively dry runout, I have a strong value hand. I will bet 200 into the 400 pot to extract value from any Ace or smaller pair that might feel compelled to call a reasonable bet.

On the river, Flashlight fires another half-pot bet of 200 after the 6 of diamonds completes the board. While they could be bluffing with a missed flush draw from the turn, their range also includes straight combinations that just got there (like any 4), a paired 6, a slow-played Ace, or higher pocket pairs. My pair of 5s is a very weak bluff-catcher, and I lose to a significant portion of their value betting range. Given the early stage of the tournament, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack rather than hero-calling with marginal showdown value.
I am on the Button with an extremely weak starting hand, 9c3h (9-3 offsuit). This hand has very low equity and poor playability. Folding is the obvious and only correct choice pre-flop.

I am in the small blind with a pocket pair (3s, 3c) against the big blind after the button folded. Pocket pairs perform well heads-up, and raising is the standard play to take control of the hand, potentially winning it pre-flop or setting up a favorable post-flop scenario. I am raising to 300 to apply pressure.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Trey of diamonds. Flashlight, in the Small Blind, has raised to 300. In a 3-handed game, SB ranges are typically wide, and I have position for the remainder of the hand. With a suited hand and being offered 2:1 odds on the call, I have enough equity and playability to see a flop. Raising would be overplaying the hand, and folding would be too tight given the positional advantage and the pot odds.

My pocket pair of threes is in a vulnerable position on this board of [4s, 7s, Tc]. I am out of position against the Big Blind and my hand has very limited equity if the opponent has any overcard pair or a draw. Checking is the most strategic play to control the size of the pot and see how the opponent reacts without inflating a pot with a hand that will likely struggle to improve to the best hand.

I have Jack-high on a board of 10-7-4 with no significant draws or pairs. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked to me. While I could attempt a bluff, my hand currently has zero equity, and checking back allows me to see a free turn card in position. Given the board texture and the fact that I'm in position, I'll take the free card and re-evaluate based on the turn action and card.

The board has paired on the turn with the Ten of Diamonds. My pocket threes have very little value here; they are essentially a bluff-catcher at this point. Given that the flop was checked around, it is unlikely the opponent has a powerhouse, but a Ten or even a higher pair is very likely in their calling range. Checking is the most strategic play to keep the pot small and avoid value-betting into a hand that has me beat.

Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked both the flop and the turn on a board that paired the Tens. This sequence often indicates a weak hand or a desire to reach showdown cheaply. While a bet might force a fold from some hands that currently beat my Jack-high, such as small pocket pairs or Ace-high, I have no equity if called. I will check back to see a free river card, maintaining my position and keeping the pot small, with the possibility of winning at showdown if Flashlight is also holding high-card air or if I improve on the river.

My pocket threes have no showdown value on this board of 4s, 7s, Tc, Td, 8h, as the board itself creates a pair of tens with an eight, seven, and four, which beats my pocket pair. Effectively, I am playing the board. Since the opponent has checked both the flop and turn, they may also have a weak hand, and there is a possibility of splitting the pot if they have air as well. Given my lack of any pair or draw, checking is the most prudent strategy to avoid losing further chips on a bluff that is unlikely to succeed against any holding that has even a slight amount of showdown value.

On the river, the board is 4s-7s-Tc-Td-8h. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked on the flop, turn, and river. This sequence strongly suggests a hand with some showdown value that they are unwilling to bet, such as Ace-high or a small pocket pair, or a total air hand that has given up. My Jack-high has almost no showdown value and will lose to all Ace-high and King-high hands, as well as any pair. While a bluff might occasionally fold out a weak King-high or a very cautious Ace-high, my previous checks on the flop and turn make a river bet look like a late-stab bluff. Given the board texture and the 3-handed dynamics, it is unlikely Flashlight will fold better hands to a standard bet. Therefore, I will check back and take the loss if necessary, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

I am dealt 7d 3s on the button. This is an extremely weak hand with poor board coverage and low potential for improvement. Playing this hand would be a waste of chips, especially given the stack sizes and the 3-handed structure. A disciplined fold is the only logical move to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Q6o and the Button has folded. In a blind-vs-blind situation, Q6o is a hand with decent high-card value. I will raise to 300 total to attempt to take down the blinds immediately or take control of the pot pre-flop. Raising is generally more effective than limping from the small blind as it puts pressure on the Big Blind and prevents them from seeing a free flop with a wide range of hands.

I am dealt 5c 2d in the Big Blind, which is an extremely weak, disconnected, and offsuit hand. The Small Blind has raised 3x the big blind pre-flop. There is no strategic justification to call or raise with such poor equity, so the only correct play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand, even in a three-handed game. While raising to steal the blinds is a possibility, J2o lacks playability and equity if called by either the Small or Big Blind. Given the stack depths and the fact that it is early in the tournament, I will opt for a disciplined fold and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 2h, which is an extremely weak hand. The Button has folded, and I am facing the Big Blind. There is no reason to play this trash hand from out of position, so folding is the optimal and standard play.
We are first to act on the Button with Ace-King offsuit, a premium starting hand. A standard 3x raise to 300 will build the pot and likely narrow the field or win the blinds outright.

7-10 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, especially when out of position in the small blind against a button raise. Calling would be a negative expected value decision, so folding to conserve my stack for better spots is the disciplined approach.

AQo is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Pro opened from the button, and I am in the big blind with a hand that is well ahead of a standard button opening range. I will 3-bet to 900 to take the initiative and build a pot with a strong hand, even though I'll be out of position post-flop.

AK offsuit is a premium hand. Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind, we have a very strong hand to either 4-bet or call. 4-betting to around 2.4x-2.5x the 3-bet size (to about 2200) puts significant pressure on Flash's wider 3-betting range from the blinds and builds the pot for value while we have positional advantage.

In Hand #9, I 3-bet with AQo from the Big Blind after a Button open from Pro. Pro responded with a significant 4-bet to 2200. While AQo is a strong hand in 3-handed play, Pro has shown a relatively conservative or passive style in previous hands. A 4-bet usually represents a very narrow, premium range such as JJ+, AK, which dominates AQo. Calling 1300 more out of position creates a difficult post-flop scenario with a low SPR. Given the early stage of the tournament (Hand 9/150), I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity rather than committing a large portion of my chips to a high-variance and likely dominated spot.

I am on the button with Qh 4h in a 3-handed game. This is a strong enough hand to open the pot from the button to steal the blinds or take the initiative. Raising to 3x the big blind is a standard opening size to apply pressure and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand going into the flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 6s, which is an exceptionally weak starting hand. Flashlight, on the button, has opened for 300 (3x BB). Being out of position with such low equity and poor post-flop playability makes this an easy fold. There is no strategic value in defending or three-betting with this hand in this spot.

AQo is a premium starting hand, particularly strong against a Button open raise. Out of position in the Big Blind, a 3-bet is optimal to seize the initiative, build the pot for value, and fold out marginal hands.

I opened with Queen-Four suited from the button, which is a standard steal attempt. However, the Big Blind (Pro) has 3-bet to 1000. My hand is too weak to continue against a 3-bet, as I am likely dominated by the range of hands that would typically 3-bet from the blinds. Calling would require investing significantly more chips with a speculative hand, and 4-betting as a bluff here against this aggressive 3-bet size is too high-risk given my stack size and position. The most disciplined and +EV play is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with King-Four offsuit. In this position, it's standard to open a wide range of hands to apply pressure on the blinds. Although K4o is a relatively weak hand, the potential to take down the blinds or play a post-flop game with position makes a standard raise to 300 chips the optimal play here.

With 9s Ts in the Small Blind facing a Button raise, I have a strong suited connector that plays very well post-flop. Given I have been very aggressive in the last two hands with 3-bets, I want to mix my play. A flat call is a solid option to see a flop, hoping to hit a strong draw or two pair+ while keeping the pot manageable out of position.

Q2 offsuit is a weak, easily dominated hand. Calling a raise from the button and a call from the small blind puts me in a three-way pot out of position. The risk of being dominated by a stronger queen or better high cards outweighs the pot odds, making a fold the prudent decision to preserve my stack and avoid playing a marginal hand in a multi-way pot.

On a relatively dry 8-high flop (8h 3s 5d), I completely missed with my 9s Ts, having only ten-high, no direct straight draw, and weak backdoor potential. As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard play. If Flash bets, I will easily fold.

The flop of 8h-3s-5d is very dry and disconnected, which generally favors the pre-flop aggressor. Since Pro checked, I will follow up with a standard continuation bet. Most of Pro's calling range from the small blind consists of broadway cards or medium-low suited connectors that have likely missed this board. A bet of 300 (roughly 43% of the pot) is enough to fold out air and put pressure on marginal hands while keeping my investment low with just King-high.

On a dry 8-high board, I completely missed with 9s Ts, having only ten-high, a backdoor straight draw, and a backdoor flush draw. Facing a continuation bet from the pre-flop raiser out of position, I do not have enough equity to justify a call, and turning this hand into a bluff is unnecessary at this stage. Folding is the disciplined and optimal play.

I am on the button (first to act in 3-handed) with A2 offsuit. While it's possible to attempt a steal here, A2o is very weak and easily dominated if called or 3-bet by the blinds. I will fold and wait for better spots to commit chips.

With 8c 4s in the small blind and the button having folded, this hand is too weak to contest the big blind. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack, as the potential value of this hand is extremely low.

Ten-Four offsuit is a statistically weak starting hand with very low equity and poor playability post-flop. In a three-handed game, even on the button, there is no tactical advantage to playing this hand, as it is dominated by most standard ranges. Folding is the correct disciplined decision to protect my stack and wait for a more profitable opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 2s, which is an extremely weak hand. The Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Pro) to act. Given the very low quality of my hand and the fact that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand if called, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play. I want to preserve my stack for hands with better equity and more post-flop potential.

I am on the button with 8-5 offsuit. While button opening ranges are wide in a three-handed game, 85o is significantly below the threshold for a profitable open, especially against opponents like Pro and Flashlight who have shown willingness to 3-bet and defend their blinds aggressively in previous hands. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

With 7h 4s in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I am out of position against the Big Blind with a very weak offsuit hand. The most profitable play in the long run is to simply fold rather than completing the blind or trying to steal.
65o is a weak offsuit connector. Even on the button in a 3-handed game, the EV of opening this hand is negative. A fold is the standard and safest play to preserve our stack for better spots.

With the Button folding, I am left heads-up against the Big Blind. Holding Queen-Eight suited, I have a decent hand to play aggressively. Raising to 300 allows me to take control of the hand, potentially winning the pot pre-flop or continuing with initiative in a favorable position.

I am holding 73 offsuit in the big blind. The small blind has raised to 300. While I am getting 2-to-1 odds to call, 73o is one of the weakest hands in poker and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. I have been playing a relatively solid game, and there is no reason to defend with such a weak hand against a standard 3x open. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

8d 6c is a very weak hand in almost all positions. Playing this hand, even on the button, offers poor equity and will likely lead to difficult spots post-flop. With 100bb stacks, it is best to fold and wait for a hand with better playability and higher card strength.

I am in the Small Blind with 2h 6h. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). While 2h 6h is suited, it is an extremely weak hand. Pro has demonstrated pre-flop aggression in previous hands (Hands #9 and #10). Calling the additional 50 chips would put me out of position for the remainder of the hand with a low-equity hand. Raising is too risky given the hand strength. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button in a three-handed game with 98o. This hand is well within the standard opening range for the button to put pressure on the blinds and utilize positional advantage. My previous standard raise size has been 300, which I will maintain to keep my range balanced. While both opponents have shown some aggression, 98o has enough connectivity to play post-flop if I get called.
I am in the Small Blind with 3s 2d, an extremely weak, unplayable hand. The button has raised to 300. Folding is the only logical choice here to conserve my stack.

I am in the big blind with 3h 6h, a suited hand that offers decent post-flop equity in a heads-up pot. Given the pot odds of 200 to win a total pot of 650 (approx 3.25:1), calling is the mathematically correct decision to defend my big blind against the button's raise. While I will be out of position, the suitedness and potential for draws justify the call.

I am in the big blind out of position against the button. The flop of Ace-high favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, meaning I am likely behind. While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4 for the wheel) and backdoor flush potential, betting into the aggressor on this board is risky. Checking is the most standard and disciplined play, allowing me to see the opponent's reaction before committing further chips.

As the pre-flop aggressor, the Ace-high flop is very favorable for my perceived range. Flashlight checked, indicating weakness or a willingness to check-call. I will fire a continuation bet of 300, which is roughly half the pot, to represent an Ace and put pressure on Flashlight's middle pairs or drawing hands. This standard c-bet allows me to take down the pot immediately most of the time when the opponent hasn't connected with the board.

I am facing a continuation bet of 300 into a 600-chip pot. My hand consists of 3h 6h on a board of 2s 5s Ah. I have a gutshot straight draw, as any four gives me a 2-3-4-5-6 straight. While my pot odds are slightly unfavorable (300 to win 900, requiring 25% equity while I have roughly 8.5% to hit the straight on the turn), the implied odds and the fact that my opponent may be continuation betting with a wide range on an Ace-high board make calling the most strategic choice to see if I improve my hand or if the betting action slows down on the turn.

I am out of position in the Big Blind on a board of 2s-5s-Ah-Ks. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4 to make a wheel straight) but no pair and no showdown value. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor is not optimal, especially given the board's texture is now more coordinated with three spades. Checking is the most disciplined and standard play to control the pot size, see if the opponent checks back, or evaluate their reaction if they bet.

The turn brings the King of Spades, putting three spades on the board and giving me a flush draw with the nine of spades. Flashlight has checked twice now, but after calling my flop continuation bet on an Ace-high board, they likely have an Ace or a draw themselves. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially hit a flush on the river without risking a check-raise on a very wet board.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h 6h on a board of 2s-5s-Ah-Ks-8d. I have absolutely no showdown value, just 6-high. The turn went check-check, which often indicates that my opponent either has a weak hand with limited showdown value or has given up on the pot. Given the board texture and my lack of a pair or meaningful draw, bluffing into a range that likely includes Aces and Kings is high-risk and low-reward. Checking is the most prudent path to minimize losses, as I cannot win a showdown unless my opponent also has total air.

On the river, the 8d gives me a pair of eights. The board is quite dangerous with three spades and an Ace and King. Flashlight has checked every street since calling my flop continuation bet. While it's possible Flashlight was chasing a draw that missed, they could also be holding a weak Ace or a King and are checking to induce a bluff or get to a cheap showdown. My pair of eights has some showdown value against missed draws (like 76 or 43) but is unlikely to be called by anything worse if I bet. Checking back is the safest play to realize my showdown value without risking a check-raise or losing more chips to a better hand.

K6s is a decent suited hand to open-raise from the button in a 3-handed game. It gives us a good opportunity to steal the blinds, and if called, we get to play post-flop in position with suited broadway-ish connectivity.

Pocket fours are a strong hand that thrives on set-mining, especially given the stack depths and the fact that I am in the small blind. While I am out of position, calling is the most efficient way to see a flop and realize my equity while controlling the pot size. If I hit a set, I have good implied odds against the button's aggressive opening range.

I have King-Seven of hearts in the Big Blind. The Button (Pro) raised to 300 and the Small Blind (Flashlight) called. With 700 already in the pot and only 200 more to call, I am getting 3.5-to-1 odds. Kh7h is a strong enough suited hand to defend the blind in this multi-way spot, as it has good playability and potential to flop flushes or strong top pairs. Calling closes the action pre-flop.

The flop of Ace-Jack-3 is very dangerous for my pocket fours, as it likely connects well with the pre-flop aggressor's range. Since I am first to act in a three-way pot and hold a weak pair that is effectively a bluff-catcher at this point, checking is the standard play to evaluate the strength of my opponents.

I have missed the flop completely with King-high on an Ace-high board with two spades. My hand has very little equity and no real draws. Since I am in the Big Blind and the Small Blind has already checked, my standard play here is to check and see what the pre-flop raiser (Pro) does. If there is any significant betting action, I intend to fold as this board hits the raiser's range much better than mine.

I flopped the second nut flush draw on an Ace-high board. Since both opponents checked to me, betting is a strong option. A continuation bet acts as a semi-bluff, giving me fold equity to win the pot right now against marginal hands, while still having 9 solid outs to a very strong flush if called.

The board texture of Ace-high and Jack-high is extremely unfavorable for my pocket fours, as it hits the pre-flop raiser's range very hard. With an opponent betting into two people on this dry, high-card heavy board, my hand is essentially a weak bluff-catcher with almost no equity improvement potential against standard continuation ranges. Calling 400 into a 1300 pot here is mathematically and strategically poor, as I am likely behind any Ace, Jack, or even pair combinations like pocket 8s through 10s. Folding is the most disciplined decision to minimize losses.

I have completely missed this flop with King-high on an Ace-high board. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, has made a continuation bet which is very standard for their range. Since I have no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw, I have very little equity to continue in the hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Qh 5c is a weak hand in a 3-handed game. With a stack of 9150 and blinds at 50/100, playing Queen-Five offsuit from the button is not a profitable strategy. It is more prudent to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand to maintain stack health.

In a blind-vs-blind situation after the button has folded, K7o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the pot immediately, or play the hand with the initiative if called.

Facing a small blind open raise, our opponent's range is likely to be quite wide. The board has double-paired with Queens and Eights (Qh-Qd-6c-8c-8h). In Texas Hold'em, the best five-card hand is used. My hand is now Two Pair, Queens and Eights, with a King kicker (Q-Q-8-8-K). Since the opponent checked, I should bet to extract value from any tens, pocket pairs, or overcards they might be holding. Betting half the pot (100) is a standard size to build the pot while keeping the opponent's range wide.

My hand is extremely weak with just five-high and no draws on a paired board. I checked to evaluate the situation, and now that my opponent has bet, I have no reason to continue. I will fold and save my chips.
Pocket fours is a solid starting hand in heads-up play. Raising to 3x (300) to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds immediately, and build the pot in case we flop a set.

I am in the big blind with Td-5d, a suited but weak hand. The pot is 400 and it costs 200 to call, offering 3:1 pot odds. Given the deep stacks, calling to see a flop is a reasonable strategic play to maintain position and potentially hit a draw or a pair.

I am in the big blind with Td-5d on a board of 8c-Kc-3d. I have ten-high, no pair, and only minimal backdoor potential. Since I am first to act and behind in the hand, checking is the standard play to see how the opponent proceeds and to minimize unnecessary chip loss.

My pocket fours have marginal showdown value but are extremely vulnerable on this King-high, flush-draw board. A continuation bet is unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I risk getting check-raised by stronger pairs or club draws. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot, keep my opponent's range wide, and evaluate the turn card.

I have ten-high with no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw on a board of 8c-Kc-3d-Ad. Given that the pre-flop raiser (Pro) checked behind on the flop, they might have a weak hand or are playing cautiously, but checking to them on the turn is the most disciplined approach to minimize risk when I have no showdown value or significant equity.

The Ace of Diamonds adds another overcard and a second flush draw to the board. My opponent has checked again, which often indicates weakness, such as a missed draw, lower pocket pair, or complete air. However, my pocket fours are practically bottom pair and have very little showdown value. Betting now would turn my hand into a bluff to fold out hands like 55-77 or random floating broadway cards, but I could easily get called by an 8, K, A, or a strong draw. Checking behind keeps the pot small, avoids getting check-raised, and allows me to either see a cheap showdown or safely fold if my opponent bets the river.

The board shows K-K-A-8-3 and my hand is Ten-high with no pair or showdown value. Since the pre-flop raiser (Pro) checked both the flop and the turn, my hand is extremely unlikely to win at showdown if I bet, and a check is the most conservative and correct way to conclude the hand without risking more chips on a pure bluff that is unlikely to succeed against an Ace or a King.

My pocket fours make two pair (Kings and Fours with an Ace kicker), which beats all missed high-card draws that would just play the board's pair of Kings. Since my opponent has checked all three streets, their range is likely weak, heavily weighted towards missed club draws or unpaired broadway cards. If I bet, I will only get called by better hands like an 8, an Ace, or a higher pocket pair, while all the worse hands I currently beat will just fold. Checking behind is the most optimal play to realize my showdown value risk-free.

I am in the small blind with King-Seven offsuit. The cost to complete the big blind is 50 chips into a 150-chip pot, giving me favorable odds to see a flop. King-high is a reasonable hand for a blind-versus-blind situation, so I will call to see the flop.

With Queen-Three suited in the Big Blind, checking is the standard play when facing a button limp. Our hand is not strong enough to raise for value, but it has some post-flop playability and checking lets us realize our equity for free.

I have an open-ended straight draw (any Ace or 6 makes a straight) and a backdoor club draw on this 2-4-5 flop. Being out of position in a limped pot, checking is a solid default to see what the opponent does. It disguises the strength of my draw, allows me to pot-control, and sets up a potential check-raise or check-call depending on their sizing.

The board is 2-4-5 rainbow, which is very coordinated for the big blind's range but leaves me with only King-high. I have no significant draw and my overcard is unlikely to be good given the texture. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity if I improve on the turn, as betting here would only commit chips with air against an opponent who likely has a piece of this board.

The turn gives me top pair (Queens) to go along with my open-ended straight draw (2-3-4-5). Since the opponent checked back the flop, their range is likely weak or draw-heavy. Betting here for value and protection makes sense, as I can get called by smaller pairs, flush draws, or weaker straight draws. A half-pot bet is a standard sizing to build the pot and extract value.

The opponent (Pro) checked the flop and has now initiated a small bet of 100 into a pot of 200 on the turn. This is a very small sizing, potentially a probe bet or an attempt to pick up the pot with a weak hand like a pair of deuces or a low-card pair. Given that I hold King-high, which still has some showdown value against this weak-looking range, and the pot odds are excellent (100 to win 400), a call is the most mathematically sound decision. Raising would likely only commit more chips against hands that have me beaten, and folding would be unnecessarily weak given the size of the bet.

The river brings a 3, putting a four-card straight (2-3-4-5) on the board. My two pair (Queens and Threes) is now significantly devalued, as any Ace or 6 makes a straight. Betting here is too risky, as I would mostly be called or raised by straights, while worse hands will likely fold. Checking allows me to pot control, get to showdown cheaply, and evaluate my opponent's action.

The board shows 2-3-4-5-Q, meaning any six or any ace would have a straight. I hold King-Seven, giving me King-high, which is a strong high-card hand on this board. My opponent checked on the river, which suggests they either have a weak hand or are potentially trapping. Since I cannot beat any pair, betting would only serve as a bluff that might get called by better hands or fold out hands I am already beating. Checking behind allows me to take my King-high to showdown, which is the most prudent action given the current board and the opponent's display of weakness.

Holding 9c 5c on the button (SB) is a weak, disconnected suited hand. Given my strategy to mostly play a raise-or-fold game from the small blind, 95s falls well into the folding range. I'd prefer to save my chips for hands with better playability or high-card value.

I am in the small blind with a decent suited hand (Jack-Five suited). Since we are heads-up and the stack sizes are healthy, I should see a flop here rather than folding. Calling the small blind to see the flop is standard play in this position.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (5s 4h) and the Small Blind has just limped in. There is no need to bloat the pot or attempt a bluff out of position with such a weak holding. I will simply check to see a free flop and realize my hand's equity.
I have 5 high with no meaningful draws on this flop of 8d Th 3s. As the first to act, the standard play is to check and look to fold if facing any aggression.

The flop is 8d-Th-3s, which is a relatively dry board. I have Jack-high with no pair, and only a backdoor flush draw. My opponent checked, and there is no reason to bloat the pot with a weak hand. I will check behind to maintain pot control and see the turn for free.

I still have exactly 5-high with no draws on a board that connects heavily with many random limping hands (8-9-T). There is no reason to bluff here when my opponent could easily have a pair or a draw. I will continue to check and give up if faced with a bet.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) on a board of 8-T-3-9. My opponent has checked the flop and turn, showing weakness. Betting here would likely only be called by hands that are currently beating me (like a pair of 8s, 9s, 10s, or 3s). Checking behind is the optimal play to maintain pot control, realize my equity, and see the river for free to potentially hit my straight.

I have 5-high on a board with a straight and flush possibilities. I have no showdown value whatsoever. Since my opponent checked the flop and turn, they might have marginal showdown value like Ace-high or a weak pair, which they might call a bluff with, or they might fold. Given the small pot size and my complete lack of equity, the safest and most standard play is to check and concede the pot.

The river card did not improve my hand, and I am left with only Jack-high. My opponent checked the river, indicating they likely have a weak range, but betting here would force me to bluff into an opponent who might call with any pair. Checking behind is the most sensible play to minimize risk and reach showdown, accepting that I have little to no showdown value against anything but complete air.

6c3c is a very weak hand, even suited and heads-up. As the Small Blind/Button, it's best to fold this trash hand and save the 50 chips rather than trying to play a marginal hand out of position (post-flop we have position but the hand is too weak to justify a call or raise pre-flop).

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand in heads-up play. Given my stack advantage and position, I want to take the initiative and apply pressure on the big blind. Raising to 300 is a standard, aggressive play to build the pot when I have a strong holding.

With pocket deuces in the big blind facing a 3x open, we have a clear set-mining opportunity. Both players are deep-stacked with over 100 BBs, providing excellent implied odds if we hit our set. Calling is the standard and most profitable decision here.

With an underpair to a low, connected board (5c 3d 6h), I don't have enough equity or value to lead out (donk bet). The standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser to evaluate their continuation bet. Checking allows for pot control and helps gather information on my opponent's hand strength.

The board is low and somewhat connected (5-3-6 rainbow), which favors the big blind's range, but my Ace-high is likely the best hand currently. Betting here is a standard continuation bet to pressure the opponent and fold out weaker hands. A half-pot bet of 300 is appropriate to maintain initiative and control the pot size.

We hold an underpair to a low, connected board and face a half-pot continuation bet. While we have a gutshot to a straight (any 4), this out is 'dirty' because if a 4 falls, an opponent holding a 7 would make a higher straight. With only two clean outs to a set and being out of position, our hand is too weak and vulnerable to justify calling. Folding is the most profitable and disciplined choice here.

Holding 6s 3h offsuit from the small blind is an extremely weak starting hand. With no potential for strong straights or flushes, and being far behind almost any calling range, folding is the most profitable long-term decision.

I am in the small blind with a chip lead of 18,300 to 11,550 near the end of the tournament (Hand 148). Q9o is a playable hand to open the pot from the button/small blind. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the opponent and allows me to maintain control of the table while looking to capitalize on my stack advantage.

83o is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Facing a pre-flop raise from the button, there is no strategic justification to call or 3-bet. Folding is the optimal play to preserve my stack for better opportunities, especially with only a few hands left in the tournament.
King-Jack offsuit is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. From the button, it is a clear open-raise for value and to take control of the pot. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is my standard sizing.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h-4c. My opponent 'Pro' has raised to 300, and I have 200 to call to see a flop. The pot is currently 400 (300 from Pro, 100 from me if I defend, plus the blinds). With a stack of 18,350 and being in a heads-up situation, the price is decent to defend my blind, although the hand is weak. However, I have been playing somewhat aggressively throughout the tournament, and folding 8-4 offsuit in the Big Blind against a button open is generally standard unless the open size is very large. Given the stack depths and the fact that we are near the end of the tournament (Hand 149/150), I will fold this weak holding to avoid losing more chips and preserve my stack.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I hold Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Raising to 300 is consistent with my standard opening range throughout the tournament and allows me to take the initiative against the opponent's big blind.

K8o is a marginal hand facing a 3x raise from the button. Being out of position with a hand that often faces domination issues and plays poorly post-flop, folding is the standard and safest play to preserve stack size, especially on the final hand.
